projects.title: OBSERV-E Accessibility Robotics Ecosystem
projects.summary: OBSERV-E is an accessibility robotics umbrella focused on helping visually impaired users perceive, navigate, and receive environmental guidance through robots, drones, and haptic feedback.
projects.showcase: Special section for GRVI, DRVI, and HRVI as one connected accessibility system.
projects.visualHint: Robot vision, direct Bluetooth signaling, and haptic user feedback.
outcomes: Reframed the architecture from robot → web app → end user to direct robot → end user Bluetooth signaling for faster feedback.
Defined OBSERV-E as the umbrella ecosystem that includes GRVI, DRVI, and HRVI instead of treating each robot as an isolated project.
Built the pitch direction around service-dog-inspired guidance, computer vision, and user-centered support for visually impaired people.

proof.robotics: OBSERV-E connects mobile robotics, aerial robotics, and haptic receivers into one assistive navigation ecosystem.
proof.cpp: Future embedded control and signal handling can connect robot state to haptic output.
proof.python: Prototype logic and perception experiments can be supported through Python-based robotics tooling.

detailCards.title: GRVI · Guide Robot for the Visually Impaired
detailCards.note: Ground robot that acts like a robotic guide companion, using perception to detect obstacles and route guidance cues to the user.

detailCards.title: DRVI · Drone Robot for the Visually Impaired
detailCards.note: Aerial support robot concept for broader environmental awareness, scouting, and overhead perception when ground-level sensing is limited.

detailCards.title: HRVI · Haptic Receiver for the Visually Impaired
detailCards.note: Wearable or handheld receiver that turns robot signals into direct tactile feedback for the end user through Bluetooth.

projects.title: Monocular Lane Detection Improvement Study
projects.summary: Compared backbone architectures and data augmentation strategies for lane detection, simplifying the original 3D problem into a feasible 2D lane-coordinate prediction pipeline.
projects.showcase: Research-style ML project comparing robustness, accuracy, and efficiency tradeoffs.
projects.visualHint: Backbone comparison, augmentation impact, and SALAD-style evaluation.
outcomes: Compared ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B0, and MobileNetV2 to study accuracy versus efficiency.
Used augmentation such as blur, noise, brightness changes, and shadow simulation to test robustness.
Presented F1-score, precision, recall, and center-distance error to explain strengths and weaknesses clearly.

proof.python: Training, preprocessing, metrics, and plotting were handled in Python.
proof.sklearn: Evaluation used structured metrics and comparison tables to explain model behavior.
proof.ml: The project focused on model performance, robustness, and architecture tradeoffs.

detailCards.title: Backbone comparison
detailCards.note: ResNet-18 baseline versus EfficientNet-B0 and MobileNetV2.

detailCards.title: Augmentation study
detailCards.note: Weather and lighting-inspired transforms tested model robustness.

detailCards.title: Results narrative
detailCards.note: Explain F1-score, precision, recall, and CD error in terms of lane detection quality.

projects.title: Reinforcement Learning Lane-Keeping Simulator
projects.summary: Built a lightweight lane-keeping simulator to compare sensor configurations and reward design for autonomous-driving-style reinforcement learning behavior.
projects.showcase: Explores rewards, crashes, lane deviation, completion rate, and simulated sensor tradeoffs.
projects.visualHint: Policy, reward, sensor stack, and lane-keeping behavior.
outcomes: Compared Camera, Camera+LiDAR, and full-stack sensing configurations using driving metrics.
Tracked reward, collision rate, off-road rate, task completion, lane deviation, and distance traveled.
Used simulator videos and metric tables to explain why more sensor data is not always better for RL.

proof.python: The custom simulator, training loop, and evaluation scripts were built in Python.
proof.ml: PPO training connected policy learning to reward feedback and state observations.
proof.robotics: The task modeled autonomous lane keeping and sensor-based decision making.

detailCards.title: Reward design
detailCards.note: Show how good lane position, crashes, and off-road behavior affected learning.

detailCards.title: Sensor comparison
detailCards.note: Camera versus Camera+LiDAR versus full stack.

detailCards.title: Demo video slot
detailCards.note: Use for the cleanest driving replay or the most informative failure case.

Validated outputs against EasyDMARC instead of trusting raw automation blindly.

avatarReplies.intro: I’m Danish Nadar. I build AI systems, automation workflows, robotics software, and security-focused tooling that aim to create real value outside of a demo environment.
avatarReplies.build: My work sits at the intersection of applied AI, robotics, and practical product engineering. That includes voice prototypes, ML systems, sensor fusion, and automation tools for real workflows.
avatarReplies.hire: You hire me when you want someone who can think across model logic, system integration, validation, and user-facing delivery instead of treating those as separate worlds.
avatarReplies.next: Right now I’m focused on AI engineering roles where I can help move systems from prototype to production and keep building stronger depth in useful, real-world applications.
